---
title: Useful Python Commands
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Example objects used in the examples below
exchange_rates = {
    "EUR": 0.92,
    "JPY": 148.5,
    "GBP": 0.79
}

phase_changes = {
    -1: "Below freezing",
    0: "Freezing point of water",
    100: "Boiling point of water"
}

letters = ["A", "B", "C", "D", "E"]
```

## Notebook 1: Basic Python & Data Structures

### Variables and Assignment

Python variables are created by assignment. A valid Python variable name must start with a letter or an underscore, use only letters, numbers, and underscores, and can't be a reserved Python keyword.

**Syntax**

``` python
variable_name = value
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: assignment-operator

x = 10
x
```

### Data Structures

#### Lists (`[ ]`)

Lists store ordered collections of values. Items are accessed by index, starting at 0.

**Syntax**

``` python
my_list = [item1, item2, item3]
my_list[index]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: list-index-example

letters = ["A", "B", "C", "D", "E"]

print(letters[0])
print(letters[1])
print(letters[2])
print(letters[3])
print(letters[4])
```

#### List Slices (`:`)

Slices select a range of values from a list. The ending index is not included.

**Syntax**

``` python
my_list[start:end]
my_list[:end]
my_list[start:]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: list-first-index-example

print(letters[0:3])
print(letters[:3])
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: list-last-index-example

print(letters[-3:])
```

::: {.callout-note title="Slice reminder"}
-   `[:3]` and `[:3]` both mean items at index positions 0–2
-   `[-3:]` means the last three items
:::

#### Dictionaries (`{ }`)

Dictionaries store key–value pairs. Values are accessed using keys.

**Syntax**

``` python
my_dict = {key1: value1, key2: value2}
my_dict[key1]
my_dict[key2]
```

**Example (string keys)**

```{python}
#| label: dict-string-keys

print(exchange_rates)
print(exchange_rates["EUR"])
print(exchange_rates["JPY"])
print(exchange_rates["GBP"])
```

**Example (numeric keys)**

```{python}
#| label: dict-numeric-keys

print(phase_changes)
print(phase_changes[-1])
print(phase_changes[0])
print(phase_changes[100])
```

::: {.callout-note title="Lists vs Dictionaries"}
-   Use a list when order matters (index-based).
-   Use a dictionary when you need to look up values by a meaningful label (key-based).
:::

### Functions

#### `print()`

The `print()` function displays output.

**Syntax**

``` python
print(value)
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: print-example

print("Hello!")
print(x)
```

#### `len()`

The `len()` function returns the number of items in a collection.

**Syntax**

``` python
len(collection)
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: length-example

print(letters)
print(len(letters))
```

#### `set()`

A set stores only unique values.

**Syntax**

``` python
set(collection)
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: set-example

grade = ["9th grade",
    "10th grade",
    "11th grade",
    "12th grade",
    "9th grade",
    "10th grade",
    "11th grade",
    "12th grade",
    "11th grade",
    "10th grade"]
 
set(grade)
```

::: {.callout-note title="When to use `set()`"}
Use `set()` to identify distinct values in a list.
:::

### Control Structures

#### `for`

A `for` loop repeats an action for each item in a collection.

**Syntax**

``` python
for item in collection:
    print(item)
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: list-last-loop-example

for item in letters[-3:]:
    print(item)
```

#### `with open()`

This pattern reads a file from a url and automatically closes it when finished.

**Syntax**

``` python
import urllib.request as u

base_url = "https://example.com/data/"
filename = "file.txt"

url = base_url + filename

with u.urlopen(url) as file:
    for line in file:
        newline = line.decode("utf-8").strip()
        my_list.append(newline)
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: read-text-file
import urllib.request as u
base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ds4e-org/ap-cs-dsc/main/data/"
filename = "education.txt"
url = base_url + filename

education = []

with u.urlopen(url) as file:
    for line in file:
        newline = line.decode("utf-8").strip()
        education.append(newline)

education[:10]
```

### Methods

Methods are functions that belong to an object and are called using dot notation.

#### `.strip()`

Removes extra whitespace (such as spaces or `"\n"`) from the beginning and end of a string.

**Syntax**

``` python
string.strip()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: strip-example

text = "  hello\n"
clean = text.strip()

print(clean)
```

::: {.callout-note title="Why `.strip()` matters"}
Text files often contain invisible characters such as `"\n"` at the end of each line. `.strip()` removes leading and trailing whitespace from strings.
:::

#### `.append()`

The `.append()` method adds one item to the end of a list.

**Syntax**

``` python
my_list.append(value)
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: append-example

print(letters)

letters.append("F")
print(letters)
```

## Notebook 2: Basic Python & Data Structures

### Pandas

Pandas is a Python library used for working with tabular data.

#### Importing & Loading Data

##### `import pandas as pd`

`import pandas as pd` loads the Pandas library.

**Syntax**

``` python
import pandas as pd
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: import-pandas

import pandas as pd
```

-   `as pd` creates a shortcut (alias).
-   `pd` is the standard alias for `pandas`.

##### `pd.read_csv()`

Reads a CSV file and stores it as a `DataFrame`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe_name = pd.read_csv("file_path.csv")
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: read-csv-example

gss = pd.read_csv("data/updated_gss.csv")
```

-   Creates a `pandas` `DataFrame` named `gss`.

#### `DataFrame`

-   A `DataFrame` is a two-dimensional data structure with rows (observations) and columns (variables).

##### `.head()`

Displays the first 5 rows of a `DataFrame`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.head()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: head-example

gss.head()
```

##### `.info()`

Displays structural information about a `DataFrame`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.info()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: info-example

gss.info()
```

##### `.sort_values()`

Sorts the rows of a `DataFrame` based on the values in a specified column.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.sort_values(by="column_name")
```

**Example (Ascending Order)**

```{python}
#| label: sort-ascending

gss.sort_values(by="age")
```

**Example (Descending Order)**

```{python}
#| label: sort-descending

gss.sort_values(by="age", ascending=False)
```

::: {.callout-note title="Display Behavior"}
By default, Pandas shows only the first 5 and last 5 rows when a `DataFrame` is displayed, .
:::

### Selecting and Filtering Data

#### `[ ]`

Use bracket notation to select a single column from a `DataFrame`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe["column_name"]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: select-column-example

gss["age"]
```

::: {.callout-note title="Display Behavior"}
By default, Pandas shows only the first 5 and last 5 values of a selected column.
:::

#### `.loc[ ]`

Select rows and columns using labels.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.loc[row_condition, "column_name"]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: loc-example

gss.loc[gss["age"] > 40, "degree"]
```

#### `.iloc[ ]`

Select rows and columns using index positions.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.iloc[row_index, column_index]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: iloc-example

gss.iloc[0:5, 0:3]
```

#### Comparison Operators

Rows in a `DataFrame` can be filtered using comparison conditions.

| Operator | Meaning               |
|----------|-----------------------|
| `==`     | Equal to              |
| `!=`     | Not equal             |
| `>`      | Greater than          |
| `<`      | Less than             |
| `>=`     | Greater than or equal |
| `<=`     | Less than or equal    |

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe[dataframe["column_name"] comparison expression]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: filtering-example

gss[gss["age"] > 40]
```

#### Logical Operators

Rows from a `DataFrame` can be filtered using multiple conditions.

| Operator | Meaning                             |
|----------|-------------------------------------|
| `&`      | AND (both conditions must be true)  |
| `|`      | OR (at least one condition is true) |

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe[(condition1) & (condition2)]
dataframe[(condition1) | (condition2)]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: logical-operators-example

gss[(gss["tvhours"] > 15) & (gss["happy"] == "Very happy")]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: logical-mask-example

mask = (gss["tvhours"] > 15) & (gss["happy"] == "Very happy")
gss[mask]
```

::: {.callout-note title="What is `mask`?"}
`mask` stores a condition that is used to filter rows in a `DataFrame`.
:::

#### `.isin()`

Selects rows where a column matches one of several specified values.

**Syntax**

``` python
values = [value1, value2, ...]
dataframe[dataframe["column_name"].isin(values)]
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: isin-example

hap_cat = ["Very happy", "Pretty happy", "Not too happy"]

mask = gss["happy"].isin(hap_cat)

gss[mask]
```

### Summarizing

#### `.describe()`

Displays summary statistics for numeric variables in a `DataFrame`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.describe()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: describe-example

gss.describe()
```

::: {.callout-note title="Default Behavior"}
`.describe()` summarizes all numeric columns by default, even if the numbers represent category codes rather than quantitative data.
:::

#### `.value_counts()`

Displays frequency (counts) of each category in a categorical column of a `DataFrame`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe["column_name"].value_counts()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: value-counts-example

gss["degree"].value_counts()
```

##### `pd.crosstab()`

`pd.crosstab()` displays frequency counts for combinations of two categorical variables.

**Syntax**

``` python
pd.crosstab(dataframe["column1"], dataframe["column2"])
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: crosstab-counts-example

deg_cat = ["Less than high school", "High school", "Bachelor's", "Graduate"]
hap_cat = ["Very happy", "Pretty happy", "Not too happy"]

deg_hap_mask = (gss["happy"].isin(hap_cat)) & (gss["degree"].isin(deg_cat))

deg_hap_df = gss[deg_hap_mask]

pd.crosstab(deg_hap_df["degree"], deg_hap_df["happy"])
```

### Grouping

#### `.groupby()`

Groups rows in a `DataFrame` based on one or more columns.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.groupby("column_name")
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: groupby-example

gss.groupby("degree")["age"].mean()
```

::: {.callout-note title="What `groupby()` does"}
`.groupby()` groups data by a column so a summary statistic can be calculated for each group.
:::

## Notebook 3: Visualizing Distributions

### Matplotlib

`matplotlib.pyplot` is a Python library used for creating visualizations such as bar charts, histograms, and boxplots.

#### `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt`

`import matplotlib.pyplot as plt` loads the plotting library and assigns it the alias `plt`.

**Syntax**

``` python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: import-matplotlib.pyplot

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
```

-   `as plt` creates a shortcut (alias).

-   `plt` is the standard alias for `matplotlib`.

#### `.plot.bar()`

Bar charts show the distribution of a categorical variable and are created using `.plot.bar()`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe["column_name"].value_counts().plot.bar()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: age-group-bar-chart

gss["age_group"].value_counts().plot.bar()
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Age Groups")
plt.show();
```

::: {.callout-note title="Using `.value_counts()`"}
`.value_counts()` counts each category, which provides the values used to create a bar chart.
:::

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: crosstab-bar-chart
pd.crosstab(deg_hap_df["degree"], deg_hap_df["happy"]).plot.bar()

plt.xlabel("Degree")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Happiness Levels by Education Level")
plt.legend(title="Happiness Level")
plt.show();
```

##### Labels and Titles

Use these functions to label and display the visualization.

| Function       | Purpose                         |
|----------------|---------------------------------|
| `plt.xlabel()` | Labels the $x-$axis             |
| `plt.ylabel()` | Labels the $y-$axis             |
| `plt.title()`  | Adds a descriptive title        |
| `plt.legend()` | Adds a descriptive legend title |
| `plt.show()`   | Displays the completed plot     |

#### `.hist()`

Histograms show the distribution of a numeric variable and are created using `.hist()`.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe["numeric_column"].hist()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: age-histogram

gss["age"].hist()
plt.xlabel("Age")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Age")
plt.show();
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: age-histogram-bins

gss["age"].hist(edgecolor="white", grid=False)
plt.xlabel("Age")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Age")
plt.show();
```

::: {.callout-note title="Improving readability"}
Adding `edgecolor="white"` outlines the bins, and `grid=False` removes the background grid.
:::

#### `.boxplot()`

Boxplots show the distribution of a numeric variable across categories.

**Syntax**

``` python
dataframe.boxplot(column="numeric_column", by="category_column")
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: tvhours-boxplot-by-age-group

gss.boxplot(column="tvhours", by="age_group", vert=False, grid=False)
plt.title("Distribution of TV Viewing by Age Group")
plt.suptitle("")
plt.xlabel("Hours Spent Watching Television")
plt.ylabel("")
plt.show();
```

::: {.callout-note title="Controlling orientation"}
`vert=False` displays the boxplot horizontally.
:::

##### Title

Use this function to label the visualization.

| Function         | Purpose                           |
|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| `plt.suptitle()` | Controls the overall figure title |

## Notebook 4: Visualizing Trends

#### `.plot()`

Line charts show how a numerical variable changes over an ordered variable (such as time) and are created using `.plot()`.

**Syntax**

``` python
grouped = dataframe.groupby("time_column")
grouped["numeric_column"].aggregation_function().plot()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: line-plot-example

grps_survey_year = gss.groupby("survey_year")

grps_survey_year["wwwhr"].mean().round(2).dropna().plot()
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Time (Hours)")
plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
plt.title("Mean Internet Usage per Week by Survey Year")
plt.show();
```

::: {.callout-note title="Why grouping first matters"}
Line charts require summarized data. `.groupby()` should be used before plotting to calculate summary values over time.
:::

##### Gridlines

| Function     | Purpose                   |
|--------------|---------------------------|
| `plt.grid()` | Adds or removes gridlines |

::: {.callout-note title="Improving readability"}
`plt.grid(axis= y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)` adds light horizontal gridlines.
:::

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: line-plot-xticks-example
wwwhr_years = range(2000, 2024, 2)

grps_survey_year["wwwhr"].mean().round(2).dropna().plot(marker="o")
plt.xticks(wwwhr_years)
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Time (Hours)")
plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
plt.title("Mean Internet Usage per Week by Survey Year")
plt.show();
```

::: {.callout-note title="Markers"}
`marker="o"` adds a circle at each data point on the line.
:::

##### $x-$ticks

| Function       | Purpose                                                |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| `plt.xticks()` | Controls the tick labels and positions on the $x-$axis |

::: {.callout-note title="Using `range()`"}
`range(2000, 2024, 2)` generates values from 2000 up to (but not including) 2024, stepping by 2.
:::

#### `.unstack()`

After grouping by two variables (time and group), `.unstack()` moves the group level into columns so each group becomes its own line.

**Syntax**

``` python
grouped = dataframe.groupby(["time_column", "group_column"])
grouped["numeric_column"].mean().dropna().round(2).unstack()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: unstack-example

grps_age_groups = gss.groupby(["survey_year", "age_group"])
grps_age_groups["yearsjob"].mean().dropna().round(2).unstack()
```

**Example**

```{python}
#| label: multiline-example

yearsjob_years = range(2002, 2026, 4)

grps_age_groups["yearsjob"].mean().dropna().round(2).unstack().plot(marker="o")
plt.xticks(yearsjob_years)
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Years")
plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
plt.legend(title="Age Group")
plt.title("Mean Years at Current Job Over Time by Age Group")
plt.show();
```
